coredata.dc:title: Construction of high-performance nitrogen-doped graphene quantum Dots@Carbon cloth-based electrode materials via cyclic voltammetry activation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yongbo
Wang, Yanxiang
Liu, Dongming
Feng, Yanqiu
Yang, Deli
Wu, Simeng
Jiang, Haotian
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have gained significant attention in supercapacitors (SCs) for their excellent conductivity, high surface area, and abundant active sites. Here, N-GQDs@activated carbon cloth composite electrode (N-GQDs@ACC) has been constructed by electrochemical deposition and cyclic voltammetry (CV) activation using carbon cloth as the current collector, where CV activation can significantly improve the distribution of N-GQDs on the ACC surface. After 2000 cycles of CV activation, N-GQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of ACC. The activation mechanism is summarized based on structural and compositional changes during CV activation, and the differences of electrochemical performance under different CV activation conditions are analyzed. The results show that the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode exhibits excellent energy storage capabilities with an area specific capacitance of 1191.67 mF cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2 (2775.15 F g−1 at 1.5 A g−1), which is 7.76 times that of the unactivated electrode. Additionally, the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode demonstrates outstanding rate performance and cycling stability (99.8 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles). The assembled flexible device exhibits high energy density (50.69 μWh cm−2 at 500 μW cm−2), stable output voltage, excellent bending stability, and good low-temperature performance. This work focuses on analyzing the evolution of the microstructure or composition of N-GQDs@ACC under CV activation and shows great application prospects of N-GQDs-based electrode material in advanced flexible SCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
ACC
Electrodeposition
CV activation
Flexible SCs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and compositional evolution of N-GQDs@ACC during CV activation
Energy storage performance of the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode for flexible supercapacitors

This work is supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province (2021ZLGX01) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (2023TSGC0545).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-GQDs, SAED pattern of red area in the upper left; (a1) HRTEM image of the orange-yellow area; (b) Size distribution diagram of N-GQDs; (c–d) AFM image and height distribution curves of N-GQDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of N-GQDs (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s; (g) FTIR spectra, (h) UV and PL spectra of N-GQDs, optical photos of N-GQDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp in the upper right. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The TEM images of N-GQDs@ACC-X: (a-a1) 0.5 mg mL−1 N-GQDs electrolyte, (b) N-GQDs@ACC-0, (b1) SAED pattern of N-GQDs@ACC-0, (c) N-GQDs@ACC-2, (d) N-GQDs@ACC-3, (e-e1) N-GQDs@ACC-4
(a) The Raman spectra, (b) The FTIR spectra, (c) The XPS full spectra of N-GQDs@ACC-X; The high-resolution XPS of N-GQDs@ACC-X:(d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, (f) N 1s.
Schematic diagram of CV activation mechanism.

(a) CV curve (100 mV s−1), (b) GCD curve (1 mA cm−2), (c) The linear relationship curve between log i (peak current) and log v (scan rate),and (d) EIS curve of N-GQDs@ACC-X, the upper left image is the fitting circuit of the electrode, and the upper right image is the enlarged rectangular area; (e) CV curve,(f) GCD curve of N-GQDs@ACC-4; (g) Areal capacitance of N-GQDs@ACC at different current densities; (h) The cyclic performance and Coulomb efficiency curves of N-GQDs@ACC-4 (50 mA cm−2).

(a) CV curves, (b) GCD curves, (c) EIS curve, (d) Areal and volumetric specific capacitance, (e) Cycling performance and coulombic efficiency, the inset shows CV curves before and after cycling, (f) Energy density and power density of FSSCs.

(a) GCD curves of a single and three FSSCs in series; (b) CV curves of the FSSCs under different bending conditions; Practical applications of the serial device: (c1-c2) lighting LED, (d1-d3) working in cold environment.
The preparation process of N-GQD@ACC-4. 1) the preparation of N-GQDs. 2) the preparation of N-GQDs@ACC-X

head.ce:title: Construction of high-performance nitrogen-doped graphene quantum Dots@Carbon cloth-based electrode materials via cyclic voltammetry activation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250061, China

State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials, State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250061, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution and Processing of Materials
State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have gained significant attention in supercapacitors (SCs) for their excellent conductivity, high surface area, and abundant active sites. Here, N-GQDs@activated carbon cloth composite electrode (N-GQDs@ACC) has been constructed by electrochemical deposition and cyclic voltammetry (CV) activation using carbon cloth as the current collector, where CV activation can significantly improve the distribution of N-GQDs on the ACC surface. After 2000 cycles of CV activation, N-GQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of ACC. The activation mechanism is summarized based on structural and compositional changes during CV activation, and the differences of electrochemical performance under different CV activation conditions are analyzed. The results show that the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode exhibits excellent energy storage capabilities with an area specific capacitance of 1191.67 mF cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2 (2775.15 F g−1 at 1.5 A g−1), which is 7.76 times that of the unactivated electrode. Additionally, the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode demonstrates outstanding rate performance and cycling stability (99.8 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles). The assembled flexible device exhibits high energy density (50.69 μWh cm−2 at 500 μW cm−2), stable output voltage, excellent bending stability, and good low-temperature performance. This work focuses on analyzing the evolution of the microstructure or composition of N-GQDs@ACC under CV activation and shows great application prospects of N-GQDs-based electrode material in advanced flexible SCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: High-performance flexible energy storage devices have become increasingly important and appealing with the development of intelligent wearable technologies [1–3]. Compared with batteries, flexible SCs have garnered significant attention for their high-power density, rapid charging/discharging, long cycle life, and excellent flexibility [4–8]. Carbon materials such as carbon fiber [9–12], carbon nanotubes [13], porous carbon [14–16], and graphene [17–19] are widely used as electrode materials for flexible SCs due to their low cost, high specific surface area, excellent electrical conductivity and chemical stability. However, their low energy density limits their practical application [20]. Therefore, the preparation of carbon-based electrode materials with high energy storage, excellent mechanical performance, lightweight and low-cost has become the research focus on flexible SCs [21].

N-GQDs, typically sized between 20 and 50 nm, are extensively used as electrode materials for SCs due to their high surface area, excellent conductivity, adjustable size/shape and rich active sites [22,23]. When N-GQDs are loaded onto the electrode surface as “conductive units” or “structural connectors”, they can increase the conductivity of the electrode and provide a large number of active sites, while reducing the volume expansion or structural collapse of the electrode during charge and discharge processes, resulting in excellent electrochemical performance of the composite electrode [24]. N-GQDs can optimize the electronic structure at the interface of composite electrodes, enhance the efficiency of interface electron transfer, reduce electron transmission impedance, and thus improve the rate performance of electrodes. In addition, the abundant oxygen or nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs can undergo rapid redox reactions, increasing the pseudocapacitive properties of the electrode [25,26]. However, N-GQDs have uncontrollable size and are prone to agglomeration at room temperature, resulting in a certain amount of large-sized graphite sheets in the sample. These graphite sheets increase the interfacial resistance of the material and reduce its regularity and hydrophilicity, ultimately affecting its energy storage capacity [27,28]. Therefore, there are still challenges in preparing composite electrode materials with uniformly distributed N-GQDs on the electrode surface.

Recently, electrochemical activation has gained significant attention for improving the electrochemical performance of materials by tuning their microstructure or composition [29,30]. Common electrochemical activation methods mainly include galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) activation, CV activation and electrochemical oxidation. Deng et al. [31] prepared NF/NiCoMn-P (NCM-P) electrodes using a facile two-step method and NCM-P electrodes delivered an enhanced capacitance of 2126 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 after 120 cycles CV activation, which was attributed to the transformation from NCM-P into highly active NiCoMn OH and Ni (OH) 2 structures. Yang et al. [32] transformed Co-S/G nanoparticles into Co-OH/G nanosheets through CV activation and the assembled solid-state SC exhibited excellent cycling stability. Zhang et al. [33] constructed an ultrathin MnO2@CNT nanosheet through GCD activation and the specific capacitance of the material is as high as 404 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1. These results indicate that the structure and morphology of the electrode material undergo significant evolution during the electrochemical activation process, whereby initially inert or weakly active components gradually transform into highly active species, thereby enhancing the energy storage capability of the electrode material. Although the electrochemical performance of electrode materials has been improved after electrochemical activation, the activation mechanisms have not been thoroughly investigated. Furthermore, CV and GCD activation methods are predominantly applied to transition metal oxides and are rarely utilized for carbon materials. And investigating activation effects on electrochemical performance of carbon materials is highly important yet remains challenging. Therefore, the structure of N-GQDs composite electrodes can be optimized by electrochemical activation, which can expose more N-GQDs on the electrode surface and provide more active sites for electrode reactions, thereby improving the electrochemical performance of the composite electrode.

Therefore, a simple and effective electrodeposition and CV activation strategy was developed to prepare N-GQDs@ACC composite electrodes for high-performance flexible SCs, where CV activation significantly improved the distribution of N-GQDs on the ACC surface. The results showed that the energy storage capacity of N-GQDs@ACC electrodes had significantly improved after CV activation. And N-GQDs@ACC-4 was obtained through a simple CV activation process in 1M H2SO4 at 100 mV s−1 for 2000 cycles. Notably, the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode demonstrated an excellent areal-specific capacitance of 1191.67 mF cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2, along with good rate capability and cycling stability (99.7 % after 10,000 cycles). Additionally, the assembled flexible SCs achieved an impressive energy density of 50.69 μWh cm−2 at a power density of 500 μW cm−2, coupled with good cycling stability and low-temperature stability. This paper focuses on the evolution of the microstructure and composition of N-GQDs@ACC during the activation process, and proposes an activation mechanism, offering valuable insights into the application of N-GQDs in high-performance carbon-based flexible SCs.

All reagents were analytical grade. Citric acid (AR, CA), Ethylenediamine (AR, EDA), H2SO4(98 %), HNO3 (68 %), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Mr = 75500), acetone and ethanol were purchased from McLean. Dialysis bag (1000 Da) and 0.22 μm needle filter were purchased from Bickman Biotechnology Company. Cellulose membrane and Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film were purchased from Guangjiayuan New Material Company. Carbon cloth (CC, Type: wos1011, Thickness: 0.36 mm) was purchased from Taiwan Carbon Energy.

N-GQDs were prepared using a “bottom-up” method. Firstly, 3 g CA and 1 g EDA were dissolved into 30 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the mixed solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then dialyzed for 3 d. Finally, the brown-yellow N-GQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying for 2 d.
N-GQDs@ACC-X electrode material was prepared by facile electrodeposition and CV activation method.

In a typical synthesis of ACC: Firstly, CC was sonicated in acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 15 min to remove impurities, respectively. Then CC was electrochemically etched using a constant potential method with a potential of 3 V for 10 min in a mixed acid solution (VH2SO4:VHNO3 = 1:1). Finally, CC was washed using deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h, named ACC.

The synthesis of N-GQDs@ACC: N-GQDs were deposited onto the ACC surface by electrochemical deposition using a two-electrode system with ACC as the working electrode and a platinum foil as the counter electrode. The electrolyte was 0.5 mg mL−1 N-GQDs aqueous solution, and deposition was performed at a constant potential of 3 V for 3 h. Sample was washed using deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h after electrodeposition, named N-GQDs@ACC.

For the preparation of N-GQDs@ACC-X: N-GQDs@ACC was activated by CV using a three-electrode system, where N-GQDs@ACC, saturated calomel electrode, and Pt foil electrode were used as the work electrode, the reference electrode, and the counter electrode, respectively. And N-GQDs@ACC-4 was obtained by simply CV activation (voltage window: −0.2–1.2 V) in 1 M H2SO4 at 100 mV s−1 for 2000 cycles. After CV activation, the N-GQDs@ACC-4 was washed using deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The N-GQDs@ACC treated by CV activation was named as N-GQDs@ACC-X, X is 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5, corresponding to 0, 200, 500, 1000, 2000 and 3000 cycles CV activation respectively.

Preparation of H2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte: First, 5 g H2SO4 was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water and this solution was added dropwise to 25 mL PVA aqueous solution. Then, the suspension was stirred at 95 °C until it become transparent. Finally, the mixture was cooled to room temperature to obtain the H2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte.

The N-GQDs@ACC-X electrodes were cut into 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 electrode sheets, and then the surface of electrode sheets and cellulose separators were evenly coated by gel electrolyte, assembling each other in the order of electrode sheet-separator-electrode sheet with the facing area of 1.5 cm2. Finally, the assembled SCs were dried at room temperature for 2 h and encapsulated with PET film.

The surface morphology and structure of the materials were obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM, SU-70, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM F200, JEOL Ltd, Japan); X-ray diffraction (DMAX-2500PC, Bruker Ltd, Japan) was used to analyze the phase composition and crystallinity by using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) and Raman spectra (Raman, PHS-3C, HORIBA, Japan) was obtained on a PHS-3C laser Raman spectrometer with a laser excitation length of 633 nm; Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor II, Bruker Ltd, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS SUPRA, Shimadzu, UK) were used to characterize the surface functional groups and the valence states of the surface elements, respectively. XPS was conducted using a monochromatic Al Kα source with an electron emission angle of 90°. The analyzed area was approximately 400 μm in diameter. A low-energy electron flood gun was used to neutralize surface charging [34]. No sputtering was applied in order to preserve the surface chemistry of N-GQDs@ACC. The XPS binding energy scale was calibrated using a sample work function (ΦSA) of 4.26 eV, based on reported values for similar N-GQD-based materials [35]. According to the relationship EB = 289.58 − ΦSA, the corrected C 1s peak should appear at 285.32 eV [36,37]. Since the measured C 1s peak was at 284.6 eV, a shift of +0.72 eV was applied to all spectra. This referencing method avoids the uncertainty associated with adventitious carbon. The survey spectrum was collected with a pass energy of 100 eV and step size of 1 eV, And high-resolution spectra were recorded at 20 eV pass energy with a 0.1 eV step size. All samples were stored in vacuum conditions and analyzed within 24 h of preparation. The 3D morphology of the samples was obtained by atomic force microscope (AFM, BioScope Resolve); Ultraviolet and visible (UV) spectrum and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy were obtained Ultraviolet–visible Spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan) and Fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, HORIBA Ltd, Japan), respectively; The temperature distribution of the electrodes was determined using a thermography system (E09PRO) with the temperature range of -30-550 °C.

The electrochemical performance of N-GQDs@ACC-X was evaluated using a three-electrode system on CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua), where the electrolyte was 1 M H2SO4 solution. In this setup, N-GQDs@ACC-X served as the working electrode, Pt plate as the counter electrode, and Hg/HgO as the reference electrode. The SCs were tested by a two-electrode system with H2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte.

The CV were conducted over a potential window of −0.2 to 1.2 V with scan rates varying from 10 to 200 mV s−1. The current density for GCD was set between 1 and 15 mA g−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed across a frequency range of 10−2 to 105 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV.

Areal capacitance of the electrode or SCs is calculated from the GCD curves based on formula (1) [38]:
(1)
C a
=
I · Δ t
S · Δ V
Where C
a (mF cm−2) is the areal specific capacitance, I (mA) is the current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, ΔV (V) is the voltage window, and S (cm−2) is the area of the active material (single electrode: 1 cm2 and SCs:1.5 cm2). In addition, the area loading of the active material is about 0.3–0.4 mg, shown in Table S1.

The energy density and power density of capacitors are calculated according to formula (2) and (3):
(2)
E a
=
1000
2 · 3600
C a
Δ
V 2
(3)
P =
Ea · 3600
Δ t
Where Ea (uWh cm−2) is the energy density, P (uW) is the power density, Ca (mF cm−2) is the area specific capacitance and Δ V (V) is the voltage window.

To analyze the capacitive behavior of N-GQDs@ACC-4 and evaluate the proportion of pseudo-capacitance, the current was fitted using formula (4) [39]:
(4)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v 0.5
where i (A) is current, v (V s−1) is scanning speed, k1 and k2 are constants. k1v represents the surface reaction control current, k2v0.5 represents diffusion controlled charges.

The synthetic process for fabricating N-GQDs@ACC-X is illustrated in Scheme 1 . First, N-GQDs with abundant functional groups are synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method, which can provide numerous active sites for the electrode and contribute to pseudo-capacitance through redox reactions. Then, the surface activity of ACC is enhanced after constant potential activation, providing favorable substrate conditions for the deposition of N-GQDs. N-GQDs are loaded onto the ACC surface through electrochemical deposition, where deposition time and voltage significantly affect the morphological structure of N-GQDs@ACC. Finally, CV activation is employed to improve the distribution of N-GQDs on the ACC surface, resulting in significant changes in the surface structure of N-GQDs@ACC. The fiber surface with uniformly distributed N-GQDs is gradually obtained through CV activation, which can form a dense conductive network with the matrix.

TEM images can illustrate the structure of N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The average diameter of N-GQDs is 3.94 nm (Fig. 1b) with an approximate elliptical shape. The SAED pattern displays a hexagonal diffraction pattern, indicating that the N-GQDs have a graphene crystal structure. Meanwhile, HRTEM image (Fig. 1a1) further confirms the structure of N-GQDs, revealing visible lattice fringes with a spacing of approximately 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the (110) planes of graphene. Fig. 1c–d shows the AFM image and height distribution of N-GQDs. The height distribution of N-GQDs ranges from 1 to 2.5 nm with an average height of 1.68 nm, which is approximately 5 layers of graphene. The surface elemental valence states and functional groups of N-GQDs are analyzed by XPS and FTIR spectra. Fig. S1 presents the XPS full spectrum of N-GQDs, displaying three characteristic peaks: C 1s (∼285 eV), O 1s (∼532 eV), and N 1s (∼401 eV). These peaks indicate that nitrogen has been successfully doped into N-GQDs. The C 1s peak (Fig. 1e) can be fitted into four peaks at 285.32 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.92 eV (C-O/C-N), 289.02 eV (C=O), and 291.72 eV (O-C=O), this can reveal that the N-GQDs surface contains abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. The N1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 1f) is fitted with two peaks at 400.52 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.72 eV (graphite N). The pyrrole ring is formed by dehydration between adjacent carboxyl groups and amide groups, which can provide pseudo-capacitance by redox reaction [40].

The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1g) indicates that the surface of N-GQDs contains abundant functional groups, which aligns with the XPS results. Notably, the absorption peaks at 1402 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic C-N and N-H groups of N-GQDs, respectively. The UV spectra (Fig. 1h) displays two absorption peaks at 210 nm (the π-π∗ transition of C=C in sp2C) and 340 nm (the n-π∗ transition of C=O and conjugated C-N/C=N groups) [41]. The PL spectra indicates that the PL emission peak of N-GQDs occurs around 450 nm with a slight red shift at different excitation wavelengths, suggesting a uniform surface state and size of sp2C in N-GQDs [42]. In addition, N-GQDs aqueous solution emits light blue characteristic fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (the upper right of Fig. 1h). Therefore, it can be seen that N-GQDs have a good graphite crystal structure, uniform size, and abundant oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface, which can improve the hydrophilicity of the material and the pseudo-capacitance by redox reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and compositional evolution of N-GQDs@ACC during CV activation
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the hydrophilicity of CC, rough CC with oxygen-containing functional groups was prepared by electrochemical etching. Fig. S2 shows the SEM and TEM images of CC and ACC. The surface of CC is relatively smooth with a regular graphite surface (Fig. S2a 1). The HRTEM image (Fig. S2a 2) confirms the disordered graphite structure of CC, revealing 0.34 nm lattice fringes corresponding to the (002) planes of graphite. The rough fiber surface with increasing grooves is obtained by the electrochemical etching method. The "oxidized carbon fiber shell" with a thickness about 50 nm is formed on ACC surface (Fig. S2b 1), which is caused by the oxidation of activated carbon atoms under current and H+ [10,43]. In order to further enhance the pseudo-capacitance of ACC, N-GQDs were introduced onto the fiber surface through electrodeposition and CV activation. Fig. S3 is the SEM images of N-GQDs@ACC-X. The roughness of the fibers without CV activation has decreased due to the wrapped surface by graphene nanosheets (Fig. S3a). The graphite sheets on the ACC surface gradually disappear after 1000 cycles CV. It can be seen from Fig. S3e that N-GQDs uniformly distribute on the ACC surface. In addition, the roughness of ACC gradually increases in acidic solutions under CV activation. The N-GQDs on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-5 show no significant changes (Fig. S3f), indicating that further activation has a negligible effect on the surface structure of N-GQDs@ACC.

TEM is further employed to analyze the structural evolution of the N-GQDs@ACC surface during the activation process, as shown in Fig. 2 . The electrolyte (Fig. 2a) is composed of abundant uniformly dispersed N-GQDs and some large-sized graphite sheets with a size of approximately 80–100 nm. These graphite sheets are adsorbed onto the ACC surface under CV activation, reducing the roughness of material. In addition, the SAED pattern in the upper right corner of Fig. 2a1 shows the graphene crystal structure of N-GQDs. The surface of the N-GQDs@ACC-0 is covered by graphite sheets with a size of approximately 200 nm (yellow area). And N-GQDs (red area) are embedded under graphite sheets (Fig. 2b), which can significantly reduce N-GQDs@ACC surface activity.

The SAED pattern of N-GQDs@ACC-0 (Fig. 2b1) exhibits diffraction rings corresponding to the (110), (100), and (103) planes of graphite, indicating that the large graphite sheets have a well-defined crystalline structure. According to Fig. 3 c–e, graphite sheets are gradually oxidized, etched, and peeled off, and N-GQDs gradually appear on the surface of fibers. Significantly, N-GQDs (red area) with an average diameter about 6–8 nm uniformly distribute in the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-4 (Fig. 2e), which is consistent with the SEM results. It is well known that such N-GQDs can provide more active sites and oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups for faradaic reactions. After CV activation, highly conductive N-GQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of ACC, forming a dense "conductive network" on its surface, which can enhance the electron transport ability of ACC, thereby increase its electrochemical performance.

The XRD and Raman analyses are conducted to further investigate the phase composition and surface structure of N-GQDs@ACC-X. All samples exhibit two diffraction peaks at 26.4° and 42.2°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphite (PDF#41–1487), respectively, as shown in Fig. S4. This indicates that CV activation does not change phase structure of N-GQDs@ACC-X. Fig. 3a shows that all samples exhibit two absorption peaks at 1330 cm−1 (D peak) and 1580 cm−1 (G peak), corresponding to disordered carbon and crystalline graphite carbon, respectively. Usually, a higher ID/IG value indicates a lower surface regularity of carbon materials. The ID/IG of N-GQDs@ACC-X is lower than CC and ACC, exhibiting the improvement of surface regularity. However, the ID/IG value gradually increases under CV activation, demonstrating the decrease of surface regularity, which is consistent with the SEM results. There are two reasons: (1) The graphite sheets on the N-GQDs@ACC surface gradually fall off during the CV activation stage, causing that the rough structure on the surface to gradually be exposed; (2) The formation of new defects on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC is induced by the etching effect of electrochemical activation. For N-GQDs@ACC-4, appropriate surface defects can help increase active sites and provide additional pathways for electron transport. Fig. 3b is the FTIR spectra of N-GQDs@ACC-X. For N-GQDs@ACC-4, the absorption peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C=C skeleton, while the peaks at 1180 cm−1, 1680 cm−1, 2854/2932 cm−1, and 630/3422 cm−1 represent the characteristic absorptions of C-O, C=O, C-H, and O-H groups, respectively. Additionally, compared to CC and ACC, N-GQDs@ACC-X shows a distinct C-N stretching vibration at 1390 cm−1, confirming the successful deposition of N-GQDs onto the surface of ACC.

The XPS is performed to confirm the elemental chemical states of N-GQDs@ACC-X. The XPS full spectra (Fig. 3c) of N-GQDs@ACC-X displays three characteristic peaks: C 1s (285.32 eV), O 1s (533.22 eV), and N 1s (401.12 eV). As shown in Fig. S5, the carbon content gradually decreases while the oxygen content increases during CV activation, indicating that the oxygen-containing functional groups gradually increase.

The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3d) can be divided into four peaks at 285.32 eV(C-C/C=C), 286.62 eV (C-O/C-N), 289.22 eV (C=O), and 291.72 eV (O-C=O). The content of C-C bonds gradually decreases, while the content of C-O and C=O bonds gradually increases. This is mainly attributed to the oxidation and detachment of graphene sheets on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC, resulting in the exposure of N-GQDs on the ACC surface. The O 1s specta (Fig. 3e) can be primarily resolved into two components, corresponding to C=O (532.22 eV) and C-O (533.32 eV), respectively. The content of C=O bonds gradually increases, while the content of C-O bonds gradually decreases, indicating the enhancement of oxidation degree on the ACC surface. For N-GQDs@ACC-4, the relative contents of C=O and C-O are 75.5 % and 24.5 %, respectively. The C=O groups can participate in redox reactions, thereby contributing to the pseudo-capacitance of the electrode [44]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 3f) is fitted into two peaks, corresponding to pyrrolic-N (400.62 eV) and graphitic-N (401.92 eV). The pyrrolic-N content of N-GQDs@ACC gradually increases with the activation process, among which the content pyrrolic-N of N-GQDs@ACC-4 is 63.95 %. The pyrrolic-N can enhance the electrochemical performance of the electrode materials through redox reactions involving protons. Additionally, nitrogen-containing functional groups can increase the electronic charge density of graphene and promote proton absorption in acidic environments [45,46]. Combined with SEM and TEM results, the N-GQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-4 after 2000 cycles CV activation, which can form a dense “conductive network” on ACC surface, enhancing its conductivity and electron transport capability. The abundant surface functional groups on the N-GQDs provide numerous active sites and contribute pseudo-capacitance through redox reactions, thereby improving the electrochemical performance of N-GQDs@ACC-4.

The activation mechanism is summarized by analyzing the changes in surface structure and composition of N-GQDs@ACC, as shown in Fig. 4 . In electrochemical etching stage, the roughness and surface activity of the CC increase, providing a favorable substrate for the deposition of N-GQDs, which can be attributed to the fact that the active carbon atoms occur oxidation reactions with surrounding protons under electric fields and strong acidic environments. During the electrochemical deposition process, small-sized N-GQDs diffuse faster in the electrolyte and reach the fiber surface more quickly. Due to the presence of functional groups, the binding between fibers and N-GQDs includes chemical bonds and hydrogen bonds. However, large-sized graphene sheets diffuse slowly in the electrolyte. The bonding between graphene sheets and ACC is mainly achieved through electrostatic adsorption and van der Waals forces. This combination is relatively weak. And N-GQDs are encapsulated under the graphene sheets during the deposition process due to size differences between graphene and N-GQDs, which leads to a decrease in the roughness and surface activity of the ACC. In addition, there are a certain amount of aggregated N-GQDs on the ACC surface due to the size effect and the functional groups of N-GQDs [24]. Therefore, CV activation is performed to obtain the uniformly distributed N-GQDs surface. In the early stage of CV activation, graphene sheets are gradually oxidized, etched, and eventually peeled off from the ACC surface due to the weak binding force between graphene sheets and matrix. And then aggregated N-GQDs gradually disperse due to the breaking of hydrogen bonds [47]. Finally, the uniformly distributed N-GQDs surface is obtained after 2000 cycles CV activation. These N-GQDs can form a dense conductive network with the ACC matrix, which is conducive to electron transfer and electrolyte ion adsorption/desorption. In addition, the abundant active functional groups can reduce the interface resistance of the electrode and increase the pseudo-capacitance of the material by reversible redox reaction. Therefore, CV activation can fully utilize properties of N-GQDs to improve the energy storage capacity of N-GQDs@ACC by improving the surface structure and composition of N-GQDs@ACC.

The CV curve is performed to evaluate the impact of electrochemical deposition conditions on electrochemical performance of N-GQDs@ACC-X. Fig. S6a shows that the optimal electrochemical performance is achieved under deposition conditions of 3 V for 3 h. The energy storage capacity of N-GQDs@ACC-X gradually improves with the activation process (Fig. S6b). Fig. S6c and Fig. S6d are the CV and GCD curves of ACC after 2000 cycles CV activation under the same conditions, respectively. The CV curve area and discharge time of the activated ACC are both reduced compared to the unactivated ACC, indicating a decline in its energy storage capacity. This result confirms that the enhanced electrochemical performance of N-GQDs@ACC-4 primarily originates from the contribution of surface N-GQDs. The observed decrease in the energy storage capability of activated ACC suggests that appropriate activation can help enhance the surface activity of ACC, but prolonged activation can lead to severe surface etching and the appearance of numerous surface defects, which can reduce its conductivity and thus affect the electrochemical performance of ACC.

Fig. 5 a is the CV curve of N-GQDs@ACC-X. The CC has almost no energy storage capacity, the electrochemical performance of ACC is significantly enhanced after electrochemical etching, with the oxidation peak at 0.47 V corresponding to the redox reaction of quinone groups [26]. And the CV curve area of N-GQDs@ACC-X gradually increases with activation time, suggesting the enhancement of energy storage capacity of electrodes. However, the CV curve gradually loses "nearly rectangular" shape due to the appearance of redox peaks. Importantly, N-GQDs@ACC-4 has the largest integrated area, indicating the highest specific capacitance. In addition, the CV curve of N-GQDs@ACC-4 exhibits an oxidation peak at 0.7 V, which is attributed to the pseudo-capacitance of pyrrolic-N by the redox reactions involving protons [22,24]. Fig. 6 b shows the GCD curve of N-GQDs@ACC-X at 1 mA cm−2, N-GQDs@ACC-4 has the longest charging and discharging time. The CV curves of N-GQDs@ACC-X (Fig. S7) at different scanning speeds show that the intensity of the oxidation peak gradually increases with CV activation, and the peak positions shift towards a positive potential with the increase of scanning speed. The peak current (i) and scanning speed (v) are linearly fitted according to formula (5) to analyze the energy storage mechanism and reaction kinetics of N-GQDs@ACC-X [48].
(5)
i =
av b
Where i (A) is peak current, v (V s−1) is scanning rate, a and b are constants. b is determined by the slope of log (i) and log (v). When b is close to 1, it indicates that the energy storage mechanism of the material is mainly controlled by surface diffusion capacitance. when the b is close to 0.5, it indicates the mechanism of diffusion control.

Fig. 5c shows the linear relationship curve between log (i) and log (v) of N-GQDs@ACC-X. The increasing b value indicates the enhancement of oxidation-reduction reaction on the electrode surface. In addition, the proportion of pseudo-capacitance in N-GQDs@ACC-4 is analyzed by fitting the current using formula (4), as illustrated in Fig. S8. The results indicate that as the scanning speed increases, the proportion of pseudo-capacitance gradually rises suggesting that the capacitive behavior of N-GQDs@ACC-4 arises from a combined contribution of electric double-layer capacitance and pseudo-capacitance. Pseudo-capacitance becomes dominant at higher scanning speeds, demonstrating rapid electron transfer ability and redox reactions at high scanning speeds of N-GQDs@ACC-4. The GCD curves of N-GQDs@ACC-X (Fig. S9) show that the vertical voltage drop of all samples is very small, illustrating a small internal resistance. In order to analyze the ion diffusion and internal resistance during the electrochemical process, the EIS is performed on the samples (Fig. 5d). All samples show a semicircle in the high-frequency region and a long straight line in the low-frequency region. The slope of the straight line gradually increases under CV activation, demonstrating that rapid ion reactions and electron transfer occurring on the surface of electrodes. The equivalent series resistance (Rs, high-frequency intercept of the semicircle on the real axis) and charge transfer resistance (Rct, the diameter of the semicircle) of N-GQDs@ACC-4 are 3.27 and 0.28 Ω, respectively (Table S2). The small Rs and Rct values of N-GQDs@ACC-4 display that N-GQDs can provide abundant transport pathways for electrons and ions. In addition, the oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface are beneficial to enhance electrode surface activity for rapid reversible faradaic reaction. Fig. 5e shows the CV curves of N-GQDs@ACC-4 at different scan rates. The oxidation and reduction peaks shift toward positive and negative potentials with increasing scan rate, respectively. This is attributed to increased polarization effects and higher impedance at high scan rates. The CV curves of N-GQDs@ACC-4 retain a well-defined shape at a scan rate of 200 mV s−1, indicating excellent rate capability and efficient electron transport. Fig. 5f presents the GCD curves of N-GQDs@ACC-4 at different current densities. The curves exhibit a small IR drop, suggesting low internal resistance. Moreover, the GCD curves maintain good symmetry even at high current densities, reflecting the high charge-discharge efficiency of N-GQDs@ACC-4. The areal specific capacitance of N-GQDs@ACC is calculated based on the discharge time, as shown in Fig. 5g. The areal specific capacitance of N-GQDs@ACC-4 is 1191.67 mF cm−2 (7.76 times than N-GQDs@ACC-0) at 1 mA cm−2 with excellent capacitance retention of 68.7 % at 10 mA cm−2. However, the capacitance retention rate of N-GQDs@ACC-0 is only 9.33 %. Table S3 shows that the mass specific capacitance of N-GQDs@ACC-4 is 2775.15 F g−1 at 1.5 A g−1. Cyclic stability is a critical parameter for electrode performance in practical applications. The cyclic stability of N-GQD@ACC-4 is evaluated using GCD at 50 mA cm−2 for 10000 cycles, as shown in Fig. 5h. N-GQDs@ACC-4 exhibits excellent cycling stability with a capacitance retention of 99.7 % after 10000 cycles. In addition, the Coulombic efficiency of the electrode is also as high as 100 %, indicating its high charging and discharging efficiency. Based on the above analysis, N-GQDs@ACC-4 exhibits a high specific capacitance, excellent rate capability, and outstanding cycling stability, primarily due to the following reasons: (1) N-GQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-4 after 2000 cycles CV activation, forming a dense “conductive network” on the ACC surface. This network enhances the conductivity of N-GQDs@ACC-4, thereby improving its electron transport capability; (2) During the activation process, new structural defects are generated on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-4, which can increase the electron transport pathways; (3) The abundant surface functional groups of N-GQDs provide numerous active sites for electrochemical reactions and reduce ion diffusion resistance; (4) These functional groups also participate in reversible redox reactions, contributing additional pseudo-capacitance to N-GQDs@ACC-4; (5) N-GQDs can effectively inhibit the structural damage of N-GQDs@ACC-4, thereby enhancing its structural stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy storage performance of the N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode for flexible supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: All-solid flexible symmetric SCs (FSSCs) are assembled to evaluate the practical application performance of N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode. Remarkably, the CV curve of the FSSCs (Fig. 6a) exhibits a rectangle shape at a potential of 0–1.2 V. And the GCD curve of the FSSCs (Fig. 6b) shows the near triangular and symmetrical shapes, demonstrating the ideal capacitance behavior and fast charging and discharging characteristics of the FSSCs. Fig. 6c is the EIS curve of the FSSCs. The Rs and Rct of SC are 8.15 Ω and 0.8 Ω, respectively. The increase of the Rs may be caused by the imperfect contact between the two electrodes and the gel electrolyte. In addition, the low Warburg impedance at the low frequencies suggests the fast ion transport at the electrode-electrolyte interface [43]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the area specific capacitance and volume specific capacitance of the FSSCs are 365 mF cm−2 and 10.14 F cm−3 at 1 mA cm−2, respectively. In addition, FSSC exhibits good rate performance of 54.2 % at 15 mA cm−2. The practical application performance of FSSCs is obtained through cyclic testing of flexible devices. Notably, the capacitance retention rate of the FSSCs is 93.7 % in 10000 cycles (20 mA cm−2), and the CV curves of FSSCs before and after cycling did not show significant changes, indicating their excellent cycling stability. The Ragone plots of FSSCs is shown in Fig. 6f. The maximum energy density of the FSSCs is 50.69 μWh cm−2 at a power density of 500 μW cm−2, and when the power density increases to 7500 μW cm−2, its energy density can still reach 27.08 μWh cm−2, Which is much higher than other reported N-GQDs based SCs [39–54].

In order to output stable voltage or current in practical applications, three FSSCs are connected in series as energy storage devices. The series device shows almost the same discharge time as a single FSSC at the potential of 3.6 V (Fig. 7 a), indicating that FSSC has good practical applicability. In addition, the CV curve of the FSSCs under different bending conditions remains almost unchanged (Fig. 7b), demonstrating excellent bending stability of FSSCs. The flexible series device can light two LEDs for about 30 min after charging the series device (Fig. 7c). Finally, the LEDs can be powered by FSSCs under a cold trap for 2 h, manifesting that the assembly FSSCs have certain application potential at low temperatures (Fig. 7d). The above test results demonstrate that the assembled FSSCs have good practical application, laying the foundation for the application of N-GQDs in carbon based SCs.

In summary, N-GQDs@ACC composite electrode was fabricated via electrodeposition and CV activation using carbon cloth as the current collector. The CV activation mechanism was elucidated based on the structural and compositional evolution of N-GQDs@ACC during CV activation. N-GQDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC after 2000 cycles CV activation, in which N-GQDs, served as the “conductive units”, formed a dense “conductive network” on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-4. This network enhanced the conductivity of N-GQDs@ACC-4, thereby improving its electron transport capacity. Additionally, the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs@ACC-4 provided numerous active sites for the electrode and contributed pseudo-capacitance through redox reactions, further enhancing the electrochemical performance of N-GQDs@ACC. The N-GQDs@ACC-4 electrode exhibited a high areal capacitance of 1191.67 mF cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2, which was 7.76 times that of the unactivated electrode. It also demonstrated excellent rate capability and outstanding cycling stability with 99.8 % of its capacitance after 10,000 cycles. Furthermore, the assembled FSSCs exhibited an excellent energy density of 50.69 μWh cm−2 under a power density of 500 μW cm−2 and good cycling stability. In addition, the hybrid device cloud light the LED lights for 30 min and work in low temperature. The design of advanced N-GQDs-based electrodes based on CV activation may provide broad application prospects in energy storage fields.

Yongbo Wang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yanxiang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Dongming Liu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Yanqiu Feng: Software, Methodology. Deli Yang: Software. Simeng Wu: Software, Methodology. Haotian Jiang: Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express gratitude to the editor and reviewers for their valuable comments on this manuscript. This work is supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province (2021ZLGX01) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (2023TSGC0545).
